data.title: KSA: Saudi Arabia Stocks Sagging In 2025
data.description:
Throw a dart at the globe, and you’ll probably land on a country that boasts a strong stock market return. That’s not the case, however, for the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia. The iShares MSCI Saudi Arabia ETF (KSA) is down by more than 10% in 2025, sharply underperforming the All-Country World Index and even the US stock market, which is a notable YTD laggard.

I have a hold rating on KSA. The stated price-to-earnings ratio is modest, but earnings growth is not all that great. On the chart, shares find themselves at key support, but a bearish pattern calls for a downside breakdown.

KSA Among the Worst Country ETFs YTD

According to the issuer, KSA seeks to track the investment results of a broad-based index composed of Saudi Arabian equities. It offers investors access to the Saudi Arabian stock market, which has historically been closed to foreign investors.

KSA is a small ETF with just $660 million in assets under management, while its annual expense ratio is elevated at 75 basis points. The trailing 12-month dividend yield is quite high, though, at close to 4%. I assert that income investors should take caution with this bank-heavy portfolio of Saudi-sponsored stocks—it's not just oil stocks.

Share-price momentum is downright dreadful today, earning the fund a poor F ETF Grade by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. It has been poor throughout much of the last year—it’s not just a Q1-Q2 story like we saw among global equities.

The Risk Grade is also weak, likely due to high standard deviation trends with the share price, along with a portfolio that is heavily concentrated in just a handful of companies. Finally, KSA has decent liquidity in light of its tight median 30-day bid/ask spread of three basis points, per iShares, and average daily volume of more than 650,000 shares. I noticed volume has been on the rise throughout much of this year.

Turning to the portfolio, the allocation plots along the top row of the style box, indicating its high weight to the large-cap space. That offers some stability compared to other Middle-East funds that house mainly SMID-cap stocks, but volatility is still prevalent. There’s also a healthy mix of value, blend, and growth.

On valuation, KSA features a 15.5x P/E ratio, which is close to what the broader ex-US index sells for. Unfortunately, the product’s long-term EPS growth rate is modest at 6.6%, so the resulting PEG ratio is by no means a bargain at 2.4x.

KSA: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

In terms of the portfolio’s holdings, you’ll find a high dose of Financial institutions. Indeed, Saudi banks command a more than 40% share of KSA, while resource-sensitive Materials stocks comprise 14%. Energy, perhaps what Saudi Arabia is most known for, is 12% of the allocation, which is a significant overweight compared to Energy’s position in the S&P 500, for instance.

Now, KSA has a nice trend of increasing dividends, but that’s one of the few bright spots.

KSA: Holdings & Dividend Information

Among the dark spots is seasonality. Now through October tends to be a weak stretch. The time to be long KSA is March and April, at least according to the past nine years of price data from Seeking Alpha.

KSA: Soft Seasonal Trends Through Q3

The Technical Take

With a nuanced valuation and bearish seasonal on this concentrated fund, the technical situation is not ideal. Notice in the chart below that long-term support is now in play at the $36 mark. That was resistance way back in Q2 of 2019, and it then became support after the 2022 all-time high. Over the last three-plus years, the buyers have stepped up to the plate near the current level, so the next few weeks of price action could be critical.

A bearish breakdown below $36 would trigger a downside measured move price objective to $26 based on the height of the recent descending triangle pattern. That’s just a ballpark target, but with a falling long-term 200-day moving average and bearish RSI momentum oscillator trends, the bears clearly control the trend. What’s more, there’s now a high amount of shares traded above today’s price, which will make rallies tough on the bulls from a supply-demand point of view. Resistance may come into play close to the $42 mark.

KSA: Testing Crucial Support, Weak Momentum

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on KSA. The Saudi Arabia fund has poor momentum, weak technicals, and the valuation is not overly cheap on an earnings-growth basis.

data.title: Hamilton Insurance Group: Attractive As It Takes A Unique Approach
data.description:
Shares of Hamilton Insurance Group (NYSE:HG) have been a strong performer over the past year, adding about 31% to sit near a 52-week-high. Hamilton is a niche player in the Bermuda insurance market, but it has a fairly unique business model given its aggressive investment portfolio approach, somewhat analogous to larger insurance companies like Cincinnati Financial (CINF). While shares have rallied significantly, they remain below book value, making HG an interesting value opportunity. With a different asset portfolio and approach to the casualty sector, HG is a fairly unique company that I find attractive.

Hamilton Group is a diversified niche insurer with a fairly even split between reinsurance, which is often domiciled in Bermuda for regulatory and tax purposes, and insurance. The company has meaningful exposure to the casualty segment. On the earnings call, management said that casualty is attractive and that HG is leaning into it. This is a notable strategy because social inflation has been a major problem for the segment, leading to larger losses than many legacy players had been expecting.

In fact, larger peers, like Everest Group (EG), have been aggressively fleeing the space. HG is moving into a market as others are leaving it. On the one hand, it is possible that HG is overly optimistic about future loss rates, but on the other hand, less competition does increase pricing power. It may be that legacy insurers were indeed historically underpricing the risk and are now just cutting exposure, but in the process, current rates are now sufficiently high to justify HG adding exposure to casualty.

Additionally, HG is benefiting from a credit rating upgrade, which opened up access to new counterparties. This idiosyncratic event may be giving HG room to profitably grow that other insurers do not have. Still, this growth merits ongoing monitoring, and I expect that general market concerns about loss trends within casualty are likely to weigh on its valuation.

Looking at recent results, in the company’s first quarter reported on May 7th, Hamilton earned $0.77 on revenue of $769 million. Gross premiums were up 17% from last year. Results were negatively impacted by $143 million of California wildfire losses, a common theme across the sector. Typically, Q1 cat losses are fairly small with the US hurricane season causing a seasonal increase in Q3-Q4, but 2025 is an exception.

As a result, in Q1, Hamilton suffered a $58 million underwriting loss, declining from a $33 million profit last year. The company reported a 111.6% combined ratio, up from 91.5% last year. This included 30.2% of catastrophe losses. Otherwise, its combined ratio would have improved by 10%, though last year was impacted by the Baltimore bridge collapse. Still, as of now, there is no evidence that its push into casualty is causing increased losses.

Looking further at HG’s operating segments, its international segment includes a presence on the Lloyd’s syndicate, as well as a stand-alone Hamilton platform focused on small and medium-sized businesses. I think it is important to emphasize that “International” includes the US because this is a Bermuda firm. About 88% of this business is insurance, with just 12% being reinsurance. Gross written premiums were $370 million in Q1, up from $321 million a year ago. The unit broke even on an underwriting basis after reporting a $5 million profit last year. Core underwriting improvements of 4% were offset by 12% more catastrophe losses, as $29 million of cat losses sat in this unit.

Hamilton has improved its underwriting performance over the past five years, suggesting its improved credit profile is leading to better business opportunities, even with challenges plaguing the casualty sector as a whole. I would also note that 2022’s combined ratio would be 97% including Ukraine-related losses. Some of this improvement has been due to lower underwriting expenses, as HG has gained scale. Still, this is a 95+% combined ratio business, which is relatively high. As I will disclose below, HG seeks to make more of its income from investing in the float rather than on insurance underwriting.

Its Bermuda segment is 88% reinsurance, which is in keeping with industry norms, and 38% is property while 47% is casualty. Q1 premium growth was 18% at $474 million, and since 2020, growth has been about 25%. Reinsurance rates rose substantially after the 2022 storm season, which has helped boost premium revenue across the sector. However, this is coming to an end. Reinsurance renewed 40% of its business in Q1, and pricing has been a bit lower year to date. Management expects pricing to show gains in the June renewals as insurers seek to add Florida cat protection after significant CA cat losses earlier this year.

This unit lost $59 million after turning a $27 million profit last year. Its combined ratio jumped 37% to 123%, reflecting 47% of catastrophe losses. Otherwise, its underwriting performance actually improved, reflecting an easy comparison given the Baltimore bridge collapse. This segment’s results will be much more volatile based on hurricane and cat activity. We have seen a marked improvement in underwriting, and I view the 2024 outcome as very strong at 87% given it was a moderately active hurricane season. It is too soon to know what the 2025 season will be like.

Hamilton has a $5 billion investment portfolio. Net investment income and gains of $268 million were flat with last year. This included $35 million of realized gains on fixed-income investments and about $65 million of quarterly interest income. It manages this portfolio in two sleeves. First, like most insurers, it has a portfolio of low-risk, fixed-income.

This portfolio is highly liquid and stable, making it an ideal source of funds when HG has to pay claims. It has about $3 billion in this portfolio. It is AA-rated on average with a 3.4-year duration, limiting mark-to-market as well as credit risk. This portfolio has a 4.3% book yield and continues to benefit from being able to reinvest maturities at higher yields given the current environment. I expect that tailwind to persist at least through year-end assuming 2 Fed rate cuts.

Beyond this portfolio, HG has a sleeve of its portfolio that is “return-seeking,” allocated to 7 funds from Two Sigma, a data-oriented hedge fund. HG has about $2 billion allocated to these strategies, and it has enjoyed a 13% return since its inception in 2012. Importantly, HG has a dedicated fund, reducing commingling risk with liquidity in five days. Returns have been consistently positive with the exception of a modest drawdown during 2020. 70% is invested in equities, though Two Sigma seeks low correlations with markets. Two Sigma earned 5.5% in Q1 and another ~2.4% in April.

HG structures its investment portfolio so that its $3.8 billion of insurance reserves are largely offset by its fixed-income portfolio and reinsurance receivables, allowing it to have this risk-seeking component. HG has about $2.4 billion of equity, so essentially one way of thinking about it is that the insurance operation ex-Two Sigma has ~$400 million of equity value, and Two Sigma accounts for the lion's share at ~$2 billion. Performance at this fund will be a primary driver of changes in HG’s $23.59 book value.

Viewing this as a ~95% combined ratio business, HG should trade at 1.05-1.1x book value, or about $25.50. The question then becomes how investors view the Two Sigma investment. Assuming it continues to return 12+% per year, as it has historically, that will be a significant source of value and capital appreciation for shares. However, given the opacity of the investment, there is likely to be some additional risk premium associated with this investment. Management is trying to take advantage of that, doing $10 million of Q1 buybacks with $112 million of remaining authorization.

Even haircutting the Two Sigma investment by 10%, HG would have a fair value of ~$23.50-$24 or just around book value. That provides 13% upside. Ultimately, I think book value is a fair multiple, given questions about the outlook for casualty losses and added risk premium for its investment allocation. Still, this supports meaningful upside, making shares a “buy” in my view, even using conservative assumptions.

data.title: Devon Energy: A Capital Return Play
data.description:
Devon Energy (NYSE:DVN) has seen some positive price momentum in recent weeks due to a general market rebound following major U.S. tariff announcements in April, but also due to the escalating conflict between Israel and Iran. Energy prices including petroleum have spiked after Israel attacked Iran more than a week ago, which creates a free cash flow upside for independent E&P companies like Devon Energy. Since the energy enterprise is one of the largest domestic onshore producers of energy, in terms of both oil and gas, I believe pricing tailwinds will fundamentally benefit Devon Energy and could potentially push its shares into a new up-leg.

Previous rating

I rated the shares of Devon a strong buy in my last work in January — A Top Energy Rebound Play For 2025 — chiefly because I liked the company’s multi-basin production strategy as well as a favorable outlook for stock buybacks. With petroleum prices surging due to the escalating conflict between Israel and Iran, a third factor could be added to support my stock rating: rising average prices and an expected boost to Devon Energy’s free cash flow.

A solid free cash flow and capital return play

Devon Energy is an independent oil and gas company and one of the largest E&P companies in the U.S. Devon Energy’s production footprint includes the Eagle Ford, the Anadarko Basin, the Delaware Basin, and the Rockies. As a high-volume oil and gas producer, Devin Energy naturally benefits from an increase in petroleum prices, which have soared since the conflict between Israel and Iran broke out into the open.

Devon Energy’s total production in the first quarter was 815 MBOED, showing 23% year-over-year growth, mainly due to production growth in the Rockies and in the Delaware Basin. The Delaware Basin is the largest production play for Devon Energy, representing about 56% of total Q1'25 production.

Devon Energy faces a major catalyst for its free cash flow growth due to Israel's airstrikes on Iran this month. As a direct consequence of these attacks, petroleum prices for WTI have risen to $76 per barrel, showing an increase of ~11% since June 12, 2025, which is when Israel began its first airstrikes on Iran. For domestic producers, like Devon Energy, rising petroleum prices are the number one catalyst for higher earnings and free cash flow, meaning Devon Energy and other energy companies engaged in production could see a considerable boost to their free cash flows in Q2'25.

In Q1’25, Devon Energy had an average petroleum price of $69.13 per barrel (before hedges), showing a year-over-year decline rate of 8%. With petroleum prices now rebounding sharply, however, Devon Energy’s earnings and free cash flow prospects have materially improved. If current petroleum prices hold steady, I see a ~10% upside for Devon Energy in terms of free cash flow this year. Since Devon Energy has considerable price sensitivity to rising petroleum prices, the higher oil prices climb, the more profound the earnings and FCF impact is going to be.

A lot of this free cash flow is going to end up in the pockets of shareholders as the company is aggressively buying back stock and paying a steady dividend, which makes the company a solid capital return play, in my opinion. In the last quarter, Devon Energy returned $464M to investors in the form of stock buybacks ($301M) and dividends ($163M). Since the energy company earned $1.0B in free cash flow in the first quarter, Devon Energy's free cash flow return percentage was ~47% in Q1'25.

Devon Energy is not only a free cash flow profitable, but very much so: the independent E&P company generated $3.1B in (non-GAAP) free cash flow in the trailing twelve months period and achieved a (non-GAAP) FCF margin of 19%. In the most recent quarter, the free cash flow margin was even higher at 23%.

Q1'24 Q2'24 Q3'24 Q4'24 Q1'25 Growth Y/Y
Revenues ($M) $3,596 $3,917 $4,024 $4,403 $4,452 24%
Operating Cash Flow ($M) $1,738 $1,535 $1,663 $1,664 $1,942 12%
Capital Expenditures -$894 -$948 -$877 -$926 -$934 4%
Non-GAAP Free Cash Flow ($M) $844 $587 $786 $738 $1,008 19%
FCF Margin 23% 15% -22% 17% 23% -4%

(Source: Author)

Devon Energy also pays quarterly dividends that will rise and fall with energy prices, which makes the energy producer especially attractive to own, from a capital return perspective, during rising oil periods.

Devon Energy’s valuation

Devon Energy, given the context of surging petroleum prices, is quite reasonably valued and although the shares have shown some positive price momentum lately, I still consider Devon Energy to be undervalued, especially to other production-focused energy companies.

Devon Energy is currently trading at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 8.3X, which compares to a 3-year average P/E ratio, based on FY 2026 earnings, of 10.5X. The FY 2026 P/E ratio also implies a massive 12% earnings yield.

The industry group price-to-earnings ratio — which includes multipliers from EOG Resources (EOG), Diamondback Energy (FANG) and ConocoPhillips (COP), is 11.3X which shows that Devon Energy is about 26% cheaper than the average E&P company. At the same time, given Devon Energy's focus on high-production plays in the Eagle Ford and Delaware Basins especially, Devon Energy has the highest expected annual EPS growth of 10% in the industry group.

In my opinion, Devon Energy could revalue at least to a 12-13X forward P/E range given its massive profitability in terms of free cash flow and shareholder orientation in terms of cash returns. A 12-13X FY 2026 P/E ratio implies a fair value of $49-53 per share, potentially implying up to 56% revaluation potential.

Risks with Devon Energy

The biggest risk for Devon Energy, as I see it, is a potential decline in petroleum prices. Lower average prices imply lower earnings and free cash flow for production-focused energy companies, which could make a share price revaluation impossible. What would change my mind about Devon Energy would be a drastic downturn in petroleum prices. Any escalation in the Middle East would likely further enhance the risk profile of Devon Energy and other production-focused energy companies.

Final thoughts

The recent escalation between Israel and Iran has resulted in serious price gains for petroleum and is therefore a favorable business development for domestic energy producers like Devon Energy. The energy producer continues to have a free cash flow upside in a world that is now more concerned about the security of energy supplies.

Devon Energy also has a history of returning more cash to shareholders, and rising energy prices make the independent oil and gas company an interesting capital return play for investors in a rising oil market. Higher free cash flows translate to increased stock buyback potential for Devon Energy, and I would not be surprised to see the company distribute excess free cash flow to shareholders in the second half of the year. Devon Energy also pays a dividend to shareholders, adding to the company’s total return-based value proposition.

data.title: ArcBest: Risk Continues To The Downside On Bearish EPS Revisions (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Intro

We last wrote about ArcBest Corporation (NASDAQ:ARCB) in April 2022 when we stated that we needed further evidence from the technicals that a new bull run was in the offing. That bull run began in earnest in early 2023, culminating in a strong bull run that finally climaxed in April 2024, topping out close to $150 a share. Since then, however, the stock has given back all that it had gained in prior years and some more when one studies the magnitude of the decline.

Remember, being chartists, we believe that ArcBest's share-price action constitutes all of the company's known fundamentals at any given point in time. If an intermediate or long-term trend has turned bearish, it means the logistics company's forward-looking fundamentals are also bearish, all things remaining equal.

While fundamentalists study the cause of the respective share-price move, the chartist focuses on the underlying effect. We believe there is a huge overlap between the fundamentals of a company and its resulting technicals, which can be visibly seen when we go under the hood of the company in question.

What does it mean by this? Well, when a company continues to report bearish EPS revisions (from a rather weak financial base), investors begin to question whether value can be created for shareholders in the long run. Although EPS revisions have stabilized somewhat over the past 30 days (temporarily ending the pattern of lower lows), earnings are still expected to slide by over 20% this year.

Therefore, to see if the recent reprieve in ArcBest's share-price decline can be trusted, let's go to the company's technicals to see if we can gain insights into the future share-price direction of the transportation-related outfit.

Long-Term Technical Trends

If we first go to ArcBest's long-term chart, we see two concerning trends that investors should be area of. Firstly, earlier this year, shares printed a long-term death cross (crossing ArcBest's 10-week moving average below its 40-week counterpart.) Such a long-term bearish signal would have resulted in aggressive selling or brought trend-followers into ARCB on the short side, hence the ugly downturn that caught hold until April of this year.

The saving grace for ArcBest has been the slight turnaround in the oscillators (RSI & Stochastics), demonstrating that we may now get a period of consolidation before a defined trend is confirmed in the stock once more. The consolidation argument holds some weight, given how ArcBest's long-term ADX trend-following indicator has fallen off in recent months.

Huge Retracement Means More Trouble May Be Brewing

In saying the above, given that ArcBest's April lows of approximately $56 a share demonstrate a retracement of over 62% from the stock's 2024 highs, real technical risk presents itself for the following reason.

When stocks undergo long-term defined rallies, they usually retrace either a third, a half, or two-thirds of the subsequent declines. The first two retracement levels are quite normal, but when a stock retraces more than 60%, there is a real risk that the entire 100% of the prior trend could be retraced over time.

Intermediate 5-Year Chart

Technical-orientated investors many times make the mistake of focusing on one indicator instead of utilizing it with other information on the technical charts. As we see below, ArcBest's rally over the past 6 weeks or so has resulted in a bullish MACD crossover on the intermediate chart. From a 'standalone' perspective, this signal will undoubtedly be utilized by contrarians due to how oversold it is and the fact that the crossover has taken place on a long-term chart.

However, given what we discussed on the 20-year chart and the fact that shares will have to surmount significant overhead resistance (as shown below), we would not advise buying into this bullish intermediate MACD signal.

12-Month Short-Term Technicals

ArcBest's short-term chart enables us to zone in on what has been happening in the stock since those April lows and what the potential for a long-term bottom playing itself out.

As we see below, shares spiked to the upside in early May, generating an upside gap in the process. However, for upside gaps to be denoted 'bullish', they should not get filled over the near-term, something which did NOT take place in ARCB soon thereafter. Furthermore, we would need to see a bullish volume trend (non-existent on the OBV) for this potential bottoming pattern to carry weight. Volume trends usually precede share-price actions in stocks, demonstrating that consolidation at best may be what will transpire over the near term.

Suffice it to say, there is every opportunity that ARCB is undergoing a short-term double top formation (couldn't punch above the $70 level with conviction) which will not be confirmed until shares break below the depicted trough. If this pattern is indeed on the cards on the daily chart, shares would first have to drop below the up-trending trend line. This may be our cue to start selling the stock.

Conclusion

To sum up, although shares of ArcBest have consolidated over the past 6 weeks or so, grave concerns are evident on the company's longer-term charts, demonstrating that a new bear market may have begun. Shares at present are fighting to trade above long-term resistance but poor volume trends illustrate that a topping formation may indeed be at play. We will update if indeed we decide to go short. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Jabil: Strategic Positioning In AI Makes This Deep-Value Play Hard To Ignore
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Observing the latest financial results of Jabil Inc. (JBL), the company showed YoY improvements in most of its top and bottom lines. However, it underperformed over the longer 9-month period and the latest trailing twelve months ('TTM'). While it shows a slight working capital deficit, such a deficit trend isn't unusual historically. Its debt profile remains stable. JBL's free cash flow ('FCF') trend continues to trend upwards. Overall, its financial standing is still manageable.

Based on the latest updated assumptions, the company remains deeply undervalued, but the margin of safety has reduced significantly.

The company experienced strong hyper-growth momentum in its "Intelligent Infrastructure", which is largely driven by hyperscalers' continued AI and cloud data center expansions. In contrast, the growths from its other segments are observed to be modest. This is due to JBL playing an important, behind-the-scenes, system integrator role for its hyperscaler customers' AI product delivery by accelerating its time-to-market and time-to-volume, making its services invaluable to these customers.

JBL's investment in the latest US facility focusing on cloud and AI data center to support increasing demands from hyperscaler customers can be observed to align with the global trend of increased friendshoring and supply chain regionalisations. This reduces geopolitical risks and also strengthens JBL's position as a reliable manufacturing partner by increasing its already large global manufacturing footprint.

The management's confidence in the company's long-term outlook can be observed from its recent share repurchases, which are also historically consistent. The total share count has been steadily declining to the benefit of shareholders.

Jabil Overview

JBL's core business segments have not changed significantly since my last article. The company continues to derive revenue from 3 main segments of "Connected Living and Digital Commerce", "Intelligent Infrastructure" and "Regulated Industries".

However, the "Intelligent Infrastructure" reported much higher growth in the latest quarter and such a high growth is expected to persist at least in the near term due to it benefiting from the tailwind of massive AI infrastructural spending from major AI hyperscalers.

Growth prospects from the other 2 segments were observed to be modest. Assuming the tailwind from the broader AI revolution can persist, JBL is expected to progressively derive more revenue from its "Intelligent Infrastructure" segment.

Financials (Income Statement)

Latest financial results show the company's latest quarter improved its revenue, gross, and net income on a YoY basis, which the management was pleased to announce during the latest earnings call that such performances were better than what they expected.

However, the longer nine-month timeframe shows the company underperformed significantly.

On an annual historical basis, JBL's recent TTM revenue has also underperformed:

With only one quarter left in the fiscal year of 2025, investors should monitor whether the company can end the year with an overall improved top and bottom line to reverse its recent declining trend.

Financials (Balance Sheet)

Working capital reduced and dipped slightly into negative territory in the latest quarter.

However, on an annual historical basis, JBL's working capital has always fluctuated between slightly negative to low. Hence, I do not see this recent dip as a material concern.

Its debt profile has largely remained unchanged, with total debt fluctuating around $3B. Both its "Total Debt/EBITDA" and "Net Interest Expense/EBITDA" remain comfortably within what, I believe, are acceptable ranges.

As I've mentioned in my previous article:

Overall, while I do not consider JBL's balance sheet to be strong, it's still manageable.

Financials (Cash Flow)

JBL's long term FCF trend has remained largely similar since the last quarter. The long-term trend has generally been increasing since 2019:

The gradient of the linear trend line suggests the company increased its annual FCF by an average of $197.72MM per year. Compared to the latest TTM figure of $1164MM, this represents an annual growth of about 17%, an increase of just 1 percentage point compared to the growth observed in my previous article.

JBL Stock Valuation

The following values, taken from Seeking Alpha and other sources, will be used in the intrinsic value ('IV') calculation using the discounted free cash flow model:

  • Starting FCF of $1164MM as referenced from the latest TTM period.
  • Diluted weighted average shares outstanding of 109.3MM.
  • WACC as referenced from GuruFocus:

  • Five-year growth: We will assume the company follows through on its historical average growth, which is updated and estimated to be 17%:

With these updated assumptions, JBL remains deeply undervalued, though less so than in my previous article.

Riding The Tailwind Of AI And Cloud Center Expansions

The company is making significant progress in its "Intelligent Infrastructure" segment, which aligns with the broader trends of AI and cloud center expansions.

Specifically, this segment experiences a hyper-growth of high double-digits:

While the growth in its other 2 segments was reportedly relatively modest:

Observing its latest revenue mix in the latest earnings call presentation slides, the revenue contribution of this segment increased from 39% in the previous quarter to 44%, a 5-percentage-point gain.

In contrast, the revenue share from the other 2 segments reduced:

  • "Connected Living and Digital Commerce" - reduced from 20% to 17%
  • "Regulated Industries" - reduced from 41% to 39%

The explosive growth in demand for AI infrastructure is expected to benefit most companies operating within the AI supply chain, including JBL.

How does JBL contribute to the AI supply chain?

While major hyperscalers continue to invest heavily in optimising the designs of key AI workloads such as model training and inference, they may not design with the end objective of effective mass production of the final products. This is where JBL comes into the picture, acting as a system integrator to bring new products to production in a way that accelerates product "Time-to-Market and Time-to-Volume" which are key value propositions of JBL as stated in its annual report.

While the company's contribution is largely "behind the scenes", JBL's offering of faster time-to-deployment of these AI solutions is crucial for hyperscalers looking to rapidly scale their AI infrastructural solutions globally to maintain or even enhance their competitive advantage.

New U.S. AI Infrastructure Site Investment

JBL's commitment to serving the growing demand for AI data center infrastructure is evident in its substantial investment in building a new facility dedicated to cloud and AI data center infrastructure.

The new facility allows JBL to strengthen its North American output for its cloud hyperscaler customers.

This move is aligned with the broader trend of "friendshoring" and regionalisation of its supply chain to mitigate the risks of global supply chain disruptions as a result of potential macroeconomic and political trends.

The new facility also enhances JBL's already wide global manufacturing footprint, enhancing its value proposition as a reliable global manufacturing partner.

Share Repurchases

JBL repurchased its shares recently, reflecting the management's confidence in the future outlook of its core business.

Total common shares outstanding have been steadily declining, suggesting such repurchases have been consistent historically on an annual basis, providing long-term benefits to shareholders:

Conclusion

Despite the recent short-term underperformance, JBL has set itself up for significant future growth by aligning its business offerings to benefit from the tailwind of the AI revolution.

The recent hyper-growth observed in its Intelligent Infrastructure segment is expected to persist, especially with its ongoing investments in regionalised manufacturing to enhance its core offerings. Its consistent track record of share repurchases also exhibits a growth that is shareholder-friendly.

Overall, I maintain my buy rating for JBL. However, while still undervalued fundamentally, the current price appears technically oversold.

Investors may consider entering at a level closer to the 20-day simple moving average, which has previously acted as a reasonably reliable support.

data.title: Alexandria Real Estate: One Of My Favorite REIT Investments
data.description:
The life science property sector today reminds me of malls a decade ago.

Back then, malls were oversupplied, tenants were struggling, and the leading mall REIT Simon Property Group (SPG) saw its share price drop from $230 at its peak all the way down to just $48.

The growth of Amazon (AMZN) and other e-commerce companies was seen as a permanent issue that justified a very low valuation.

But fast forward five years, and SPG has now more than quadrupled investors' money, proving the market wrong. Eventually, the cycle of oversupply ended, the market regained balance, and SPG regained a more typical valuation multiple:

This brings me to Alexandria Real Estate (NYSE:ARE).

The leading life science REIT has seen its share price drop from $223 at its peak to just $72 today. The sector is oversupplied, and just like in the case of malls, the REIT market has become very pessimistic, expecting significant long-term pain.

We think that this is yet another opportunity to win big in the coming years as the market regains its senses and Alexandria recovers.

Below, we discuss the near-term headwinds and the long-term opportunity for investors:

The Near-Term Challenges

The market expects significant pain because of the historic surge in both new supply and interest rates happening simultaneously.

But we actually think that the impact of both will be very muted.

Let's first discuss the oversupply.

It resulted from the pandemic and the ultra-low interest rates that followed. It initially led to a boom in new demand for life science space, so developers saw an opportunity to capitalize on this demand by building new properties. At the same time, a lot of office building owners were facing growing vacancies due to remote work policies and sought to convert offices into labs to capitalize on the strong demand.

But shortly after these many projects were launched, the demand side cooled down. The massive R&D spend to create COVID-related drugs came back down, the world gradually returned to normal, and interest rates surged.

Suddenly, the demand side was not quite as strong anymore, and that's when the big wave of new supply hit the market.

See the historic spike of 2024:

Higher supply, coupled with weaker demand, resulted in historically high vacancy rates. Many newly developed or redeveloped properties are sitting empty, and this is a particularly big issue for a lot of the office-to-lab conversion projects, which often result in a lower quality product that's hard to fill.

This hurts Alexandria because it's more difficult to secure new leases with favorable terms when numerous properties are competing for the same customers. Even if you own the best assets, as is the case with Alexandria, you will still face pricing pressure from competitors.

However, the good news is that Alexandria has an average of eight years remaining on its leases, and therefore, the impact is very limited. By the time most of its leases expire, the market conditions will likely be much better.

Moreover, most of its properties are truly irreplaceable, Class A buildings in research clusters, and the supply of such properties is limited by their locations and superior physical characteristics. They are not in direct competition with most of this new supply, which is inferior in quality and often fails to meet the requirements expected by Alexandria's tenants.

Finally, even as leases gradually expire, tenants are typically choosing to stay in their current properties because it would be very expensive and impractical to move to another facility. These tenants often invest substantial amounts of money into their leased properties, and moving all that equipment, intellectual property, and talent would be very disruptive.

Alexandria also has the best reputation as the landlord of choice in this space. Big pharma companies will commonly prefer to work with an established, reputable player like Alexandria rather than a small, opportunistic landlord who's trying to develop or redevelop a property but has limited experience.

So the impact of the oversupply is not that significant.

What about the surge in interest rates?

Again, the impact is very modest because Alexandria has low leverage with a 35% LTV, nearly all of which has a fixed rate, and its debt maturities are exceptionally long at 12 years on average. In fact, its debt maturities are the longest of any major REIT:

So, no significant impact from oversupply, nor from rate hikes.

This explains why Alexandria remains resilient even in today's challenging environment.

Yes, some pain is expected, but it's very modest.

Its cash flow grew by 8.5% in 2022, 6.5% in 2023, 5.6% in 2024, and it is only expected to drop by 2.4% in 2025:

This means that its cash flow will still be much higher at the end of 2025 than it was in early 2022 when its share price was three times higher.

I would add that the company's cash flow growth could be even stronger if it decided not to sell non-core assets to reinvest in its Class A development projects. Those transactions are dilutive to FFO per share in the near term, but will position the company for an even better future.

Despite the headwinds from oversupply, higher rates, and asset disposals, analysts still expect the company to return to positive growth in 2026.

Yet, the market has priced it at 7x FFO as if its cash flow were about to collapse. It just doesn't make sense.

The Long-Term Opportunity

So far, we have established that Alexandria will suffer in the near term, just not nearly as much as the market appears to think.

But even more important is how these assets will perform over the long run?

The two most important questions to answer here are:

  • Will oversupply remain persistent forever?
  • Will the demand for these properties continue to grow?

The first question is easy to answer. Just look again at the chart that we shared earlier. You can already see a steep drop in new supply for 2025, and it will likely only drop further in 2026:

This is perfectly normal. As developers get hurt, they scale back new projects. They don't want to build anything new if the sector is already oversupplied and the risk-to-reward is poor.

The same applies to office-to-lab conversions. Office landlords have now learned the hard way that these projects are very costly and time-consuming, with so far poor results. It typically results in lower-quality products in an inferior location, making them difficult to lease. Alexandria has previously referred to some of these empty conversions as "zombie buildings" because, in their view, they are unlikely to ever find a tenant. Labs have very unique physical characteristics in terms of their structural load capacity, floor-to-floor height, mechanical, electrical, and plumbing systems, etc., and that's why 90%-plus of offices just cannot be converted, especially not in a way that would be financially rewarding.

Moreover, the office market has now begun to recover with more and more companies returning to the office, making such speculative redevelopment projects even less appealing. Why sit on an empty building for 2–3 years to go through a very costly and complicated redevelopment for unpredictable results when you could potentially just find an office tenant and earn a decent return?

So no, the high supply is unlikely to remain a persistent issue. The new supply has already come down significantly and will remain low until fundamentals improve, and new projects become rewarding again.

What about the demand side?

It has come down from the boom years of the pandemic, when VC funding for biotech was reaching record highs.

This VC money and IPOs for biotech companies tightened sharply as the world returned to normal and interest rates surged.

This was a necessary reset after unusually strong years.

But going forward, the demand will return to its secular long-term growth that's driven by the fact that the world's population is aging at a rapid pace, and yet, 90%-plus of the 10,000 discovered diseases don't have any treatments yet:

This is a massive market and a significant opportunity for many companies to generate substantial profits by discovering valuable new treatments, especially as the global population continues to age, which only increases the need for such treatments.

Therefore, the demand for lab space is likely to continue growing over the long run as more companies enter this space in an attempt to earn profits.

And I think that this demand growth could accelerate greatly in the coming years as a result of the AI revolution.

It will break barriers to entry, lead to more biotech business formations, more competition for space, faster research, and a much greater need for labs to conduct more and more experiments. According to ChatGPT:

The AI revolution is removing bottlenecks in biotech:

  • Faster drug discovery through in-silico (computer-simulated) modeling.

  • Smarter clinical trial design using real-world data and machine learning.

  • Enhanced target identification via genomics and proteomics analysis.

  • Automation of lab workflows and synthetic biology design.

AI doesn’t replace the need for wet labs — it supercharges the volume and velocity of lab work, making the demand for high-throughput, modular lab space even stronger.

So with that in mind, it seems very likely that demand for high-quality life science will grow significantly over the long run.

The combination of an aging population, huge unmet medical needs, and an AI-fueled biotech renaissance will drive long-term demand for lab space.

Alexandria and its well-located, AI-ready, modern, mega-campus life science buildings are very well positioned to benefit from these long-term trends, yet it trades as if it were facing poor long-term prospects.

The management is also showing confidence in this by buying back shares. They previously authorized a $500 million buyback program, and they have already used nearly half of it. Buybacks will likely only accelerate following the most recent dip.

Closing Note

This is a classic case of short-term pain for long-term gain, in my opinion.

These are truly exceptional assets, and the best proof of that is perhaps their historic same property NOI growth. They have managed to grow it by 6% annually on average over the past 10 years, which is one of the highest of any property sector:

This strong performance explains why ARE has, during most times, traded at very high valuation multiples.

It is well-deserved given that its properties enjoy strong barriers to entry, benefit from secular demand growth, and could be among the biggest winners from AI in the REIT space.

Even then, everyone is today focused on the next year or two, which will be more challenging due to the current oversupply.

This will pass, and as the focus switches back to the long-term opportunity, I expect a repricing at a materially higher multiple. Even at 20x FFO, I think that Alexandria would be very reasonably valued and that would triple its current share price. While we wait, we earn a 7.3% dividend yield that's safely covered and growing. I don't know any other blue-chip type REIT with a strong BBB+ rated balance sheet, Class A assets, secular tailwinds, and an exceptional track record that offers as much upside potential, and for these reasons, Alexandria is one of our favorite REIT picks for the coming decade at High Yield Landlord.

I can't predict what the bottom will be or when the recovery will take place, but we will happily keep accumulating more shares in many phases as long as it keeps trading at such low valuations.

data.title: 5 Stocks I'm Buying As The Economy Slumps Toward Stagflation
data.description:
I have to be honest with you.

There's no coherent throughline to the menagerie of topics that follow. Instead, this week's article is a veritable hydra of subject matters, each of which, I believe, is worthy of paying attention to.

In these weekly articles, I do my best to highlight important information for investors, especially dividend investors, to absorb and ruminate upon.

As usual, after the mish-mash of topics covered below, I'll also discuss my buy list, which includes 5 dividend growers but also a handful of attractively priced preferred stocks of investment grade-rated REITs.

Here's where we're going today:

  • The Fed's economic projections are morphing into a stagflationary outlook.
  • Why most of the negative impact of the tariffs has yet to be felt.
  • How Big Tech is driving the economy forward even in the midst of multiple headwinds.
  • Stock prices and earnings expectations are going up, so does that mean the market is more or less efficient?
  • A recent Lazard report finds, again, that unsubsidized renewables are cost-competitive with even the cheapest forms of conventional energy. I'm not worried about utility-scale renewables.
  • Some thoughts on my dividend growth stock buy list and a handful of REIT preferred stocks on my radar.

Onward!

Reading The Fed's Tea Leaves

The June Federal Reserve meeting delivered exactly what the market expected: no rate cut. In fact, the Fed now projects only two 25-basis point rate cuts in the second half of this year.

Interestingly, the Fed's other economic forecasts for the economy also shifted somewhat from their March meeting to their June meeting.

In short, the Fed now expects lower real GDP growth, higher inflation, and slightly higher unemployment for 2025-2026 relative to their March projections. They also project a slightly slower pace of rate-cutting over the next few years.

Fed Chairman Powell commented during the press conference Q&A that there have been some noticeable price increases for certain tariff-impacted goods that have already shown up in the data, but due to the nature of supply chains, most of the impact is still ahead of us.

A very recent study by researchers from Harvard University and the University of San Andres (in Argentina) compared real-time retail pricing data with country-of-origin information to determine the product-level effects of tariffs. Their findings are consistent with what almost any economist would have told you.

As of June 1st:

Our analysis reveals rapid pricing responses, though their magnitude remains modest relative to the announced tariff rates and varies by country of origin. Both imported and domestic goods are affected, suggesting broader pricing and supply chain spillovers.

... These findings underscore the wide-ranging impact of trade policy, which can influence retail prices far beyond the specific goods targeted by tariffs.

It is an ongoing study that will be updated over time to show the exact inflationary impact of the tariffs.

Most of the Tariff Impact Is Still to Come

Speaking of the tariffs, here's a friendly reminder that tariffs are a net negative for the economy in virtually every measurable way.

(Sometimes they are deemed necessary for geopolitical or national security reasons, but that doesn't make them a net positive for the economy.)

When it comes to the negative economic impact of the tariffs, the worst is yet to come. And those negative effects won't be confined to relatively minor price increases in a few unimportant corners of the economy. Whenever you disrupt the stasis of a market-driven economy with some government intervention, there are consequences, often unforeseeable ones.

Let's just look at the (far more muted) tariffs from Trump's first term as an example.

Prior to the initial implementation of tariffs in September 2018, there was a burst of economic activity and industrial production to front-run those tariffs. The same burst of activity occurred in the opening months of this year, as you can see below.

But after that short burst of growth in industrial production (which includes manufacturing but also mining and utility output), a distinct slump followed it. For most of 2019, U.S. industrial production was contracting.

U.S. manufacturing entered its own recession in 2019. Even before the pandemic began, manufacturing output was plummeting.

It isn't just the industrial side of the economy. You can see the effect across virtually the entire economy.

For example, take a look at how the year-over-year growth of nonfarm payrolls as well as both the manufacturing and services PMI indices reacted to the tariffs in 2018:

It is striking how clear the pivot was from the period before the tariffs to the period following the tariffs.

It's still early days, which has probably lulled a lot of market participants into complacency, but I see little reason why the effect of tariffs on the U.S. economy would be significantly better today than it was in 2018-2019.

Already, over the last few years and certainly the last several months, the same measurements of economic health have been pretty weak:

Labor force growth of ~1% is undeniably soft, although it would be fair to point out that the downward trajectory has been very gradual.

As for the Institute for Supply Management's survey data, it is interesting to see the pre-tariff manufacturing bump earlier this year followed by its current slide. The services PMI didn't even get a bump. It has been sliding all year and recently slipped into contractionary (<50) territory.

Some corroborating evidence for this survey data is TSA throughput, the 3-month average of which recently dipped into contraction for the first time in several years.

As you can see, this drop in air travel traffic isn't strictly due to people suddenly becoming cautious in the midst of scary headlines. It is the continuation of a multi-year trend.

My take on all of this is that the U.S. economy is in a somewhat fragile state right now, and most economic indicators are likely to get worse (due largely but not entirely to tariffs) before they eventually get better.

The Core Drivers of GDP Growth Today

Above, I mentioned that I see little reason to believe that relatively minor tariffs would cause an economic downturn in 2019 while far larger tariffs won't cause a downturn today.

But, to be fair, there is one major difference in the U.S. economy between 2019 and today, and it relates to our technology sector -- the crown jewel of the American economy.

In Europe, central bank policy rates are going down. The Swiss National Bank just cut its key rate back to zero for the first time since 2022. And yet, here in the U.S., interest rates remain stubbornly elevated (at least relative to the last decade and a half).

Why?

Part of the reason is slightly higher inflation expectations, as explained above.

But another reason is the fact that U.S. real GDP growth has remained remarkably resilient.

I would argue that service-based economies are less susceptible to cyclical swings than agriculture- or manufacturing-based economies. But there's more to it than that.

Despite tariffs and incendiary geopolitics, most economists, including Torsten Slok, chief economist of Apollo Global Management (APO), foresee only a slowdown this year instead of a recession.

Service-based, market-driven economies with decent demographics tend to be pretty stable, but the U.S. economy has arguably been better than stable. American economic growth has been the envy of the world.

I'd argue that the American innovation ecosystem broadly and AI-related capital expenditures specifically have been the core driver of growth over the last several quarters.

The use of AI tools like ChatGPT is accelerating at a rapid pace.

And that is pushing Big Tech hyperscalers like Amazon (AMZN), Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), Microsoft (MSFT), and Meta Platforms (META) to allocate ever greater levels of capital to grow the physical and digital infrastructure required to run these AI tools.

These four hyperscalers alone are now spending over $70 billion per quarter on capex, with most of that associated with AI buildout.

There have even been reports that Meta's Mark Zuckerberg has offered compensation packages upwards of $100 million to poach top AI talent from other companies like OpenAI.

The level of investment in this area is incredible.

In Q1, investments into IT equipment (in the present case, data centers) contributed an astounding 100 basis points to GDP growth.

That is a greater contribution than the quarterly peak during the Internet boom of the late 1990s and early 2000s.

So, between the stability of a service-based, market-driven economy and unprecedented levels of technology investment, U.S. GDP growth does seem likely to keep powering forward, albeit at a slower pace.

Unless the trade war situation re-escalates, it now appears as though the U.S. economy will either avoid a recession or experience only a shallow one.

Stock Prices And Expected Earnings Growth Going Up

Lots of folks are commenting on how surprisingly buoyant the stock market has been in the midst of all the economic risks and scary headlines.

The S&P 500's (SP500, SPY) forward P/E ratio has rebounded above one standard deviation higher than its 30-year average level of 17x:

It is interesting that the SPY bounced right off that +1 standard deviation level after its April selloff. But I'm no market technician, and I should quickly back away from that topic before delving any further.

You can see from the box within the chart above that by basically every valuation metric, the SPY is well above its 30-year average levels. Price to cash flow, for example, was 44% higher than its 30-year average at the end of May.

On the other hand, a higher valuation makes sense if earnings growth justifies it. And after the post-"Liberation Day" mark-down of earnings expectations, the analyst consensus projection for SPY earnings over the next 12 months has now rebounded back above its pre-"Liberation Day" peak.

Further complicating the situation is the fact that the Magnificent 7 (MAGS) mega-cap stocks account for an outsized share of this expected earnings growth relative to the rest of the market:

The delta between the Mag 7's earnings growth and that of the rest of the market is shrinking, but it is still pretty big.

And, perhaps appropriately, the Mag 7's price performance over the last year has outperformed that of the equal-weight S&P 500 (RSP) by about the same amount as its expected earnings growth outperformance.

While the market is never perfectly efficient, it does appear to be efficiently valuing the Mag 7 relative to the rest of the market, at least assuming the analyst consensus earnings estimates are accurate.

So, maybe the market isn't that overvalued? Maybe the market is appropriately valuing the world's best innovation machines?

Okay, maybe Big Tech companies really are that awesome. Let's assume so, for the sake of argument.

How does that explain the rich valuation of the rest of the market outside the Mag 7 (or, in the chart below, the top 10 largest stocks)?

As you can see, all stocks outside of the top 10 largest are valued at about 19% above their 30-year average level.

Maybe the market anticipates that the users of AI (everything outside of Big Tech) will eventually be winners from this new technology as they incorporate it into their businesses and become more efficient and profitable.

Or maybe the world's affluent simply have tons of investable cash and see no better place to allocate it than US stocks.

Or maybe, as Josh Brown of Ritholtz Wealth Management has argued on his YouTube channel, American companies have become better managed and more profitable than ever before and therefore deserve higher valuations.

All of these arguments are logical in and of themselves.

But one should also consider the market's very long history of overshooting in one direction or another.

You know what they say about reversion to the mean.

Yes, Renewable Energy Is Cost-Competitive Without Subsidies

Details about the U.S. Senate's version of the congressional tax-and-spend bill recently emerged and caused quite a stir among green energy stocks, especially solar companies (TAN).

The House of Representatives version of the bill rapidly phased out tax credits for all aspects of green energy (development, production, and manufacturing), requiring projects to start within 60 days of bill signing and be completed by the end of 2028. The Senate's version of the bill slightly modified these provisions in a way that, to my mind, makes more sense while still phasing out subsidies fairly quickly.

In the Senate's modification of the bill:

  • The residential solar tax credit of 30% will expire 180 days after the passage & signing of the bill.
  • Solar and wind power have a longer runway to begin construction and still receive the tax credits: 100% if construction begins in 2025, 60% in 2026, 20% in 2027, and 0% in 2028 or after. This pulls forward and speeds up the expiration of the tax credits from the IRA, which wouldn't have been phased out until 2032.
  • New battery storage, nuclear, hydropower, and geothermal plants still have full tax credits through 2033, but the credits will then be phased out from 2034 to 2036.
  • Existing hydropower plants did not receive an extension of tax credits, which could have a detrimental effect on older dams that are in need of maintenance and upgrades.
  • Tax credits for new and used EVs would end six months after the signing of the bill.
  • Critically, the tax credits remain transferable, which helps developers with the upfront financing of new projects.

It is significant that the Senate's modification establishes construction start deadlines rather than in-service deadlines because renewable energy developers have limited control over when completed projects are connected to the grid and begin commercial operations. There are often long interconnection queues that make it difficult to predict exactly when a project will be placed into service.

The Senate bill looks very bad (worse than expected) for residential solar (hence the sudden plunge in many solar stocks).

But I'd argue that utility-scale renewables like wind, solar, and battery storage are going to be just fine. Growth will probably be slower than if the tax credits remained, as developer financing will become incrementally more expensive after this year. But renewables can and will still play a valuable role in meeting the country's growing power needs going forward.

The most recently released Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE) report from Lazard provides more support for this thesis, as it finds new-build renewables cost-competitive with marginal use of conventional sources of power generation like gas and nuclear.

To quote the executive summary of the report:

On an unsubsidized $/MWh basis, renewable energy remains the most cost-competitive form of generation. As such, renewable energy will continue to play a key role in the buildout of new power generation in the U.S. This is particularly true in the current high power demand environment, where renewables stand out as both the lowest-cost and quickest-to-deploy generation resource.

It is true that renewables' LCOEs have rebounded slightly in recent years as a result of both higher capital costs and increased domestic sourcing of inputs (as opposed to cheaper international suppliers).

But as the management of NextEra Energy Inc. (NEE) has been attesting on their last few earnings conference calls, the construction and capex costs of conventional power plants have risen considerably in recent years as well.

Quoting the Lazard report again:

[W]hile this year’s analysis shows only a slight increase in the LCOE of CCGTs, turbine shortages, rising costs, and long lead times are expected to drive steep LCOE increases for gas technologies in the near term, as illustrated herein.

On the other hand, Lazard's report does find that the increased penetration of intermittent renewables in the grid is causing the cost of dispatchable power to increase, which limits the extent to which intermittent sources' share of power generation can or should rise.

Again quoting the report:

Despite the sustained unsubsidized cost competitiveness of renewable energy, resource planning metrics indicate diverse generation fleets will be required over the long term to meet power needs, likely bolstered by now-emerging technologies such as long-duration energy storage, geothermal, nuclear small modular reactors, pumped storage hydropower and carbon capture and storage, among others.

Like it or not, we are living in an "all of the above" energy world. The economy's needs cannot be sufficiently met by either fossil fuels or renewables on their own.

That's bad news for both pro-decarbonization and anti-renewables folks, but I'd argue it's good news for my fellow shareholders of renewable power producers like NextEra Energy, Brookfield Renewable (BEP, BEPC), and Clearway Energy Inc. (CWEN, CWEN.A).

My Dividend Stock Buy List

Without further ado, let's get to my current buy list, which is really two buy lists -- one made up of dividend growers and another of REIT preferred stocks.

First, the dividend growers:

Dividend Yield Projected Dividend Growth Rate (Guesstimate)
American Homes 4 Rent (AMH) 3.3% Mid- to High-Single-Digits
Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) 3.3% Mid- to High-Single-Digits
Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA) 4.1% Mid-Single-Digits
Public Storage (PSA) 4.1% Mid-Single-Digits
Rexford Industrial (REXR) 4.7% Mid-Single-Digits


Four of these 5 stocks were on my buy list from last week's article, titled "Why I Will Never Retire," so to avoid repetition, I'll point you to that article for my thoughts on these excellent REITs. The 5th company, Public Storage, is much like its other four REIT brethren on this list in that it boasts a market-leading position in its space, a fortress balance sheet, a high credit rating, and a long history of steady compounding.

That's the core of my investment strategy: Invest in high-quality dividend growers capable of steadily and reliably compounding cash flows indefinitely.

I try to studiously avoid both high-yielders and high-upside plays, and I'm suspicious of any stock that would need to grow at a 15% annual rate (or more) for the valuation to make sense.

A moderate yield and moderate growth at a reasonable (or better) price is my sweet spot. That's the most reliable way to generate long-term wealth, in my opinion.

I think PSA sold off recently in sympathy with homebuilders (XHB) and homebuilder-adjacent stocks like Home Depot (HD) after a poor homebuilder sentiment reading. Like HD, a lot of PSA's core customer demand comes from normal levels of housing turnover, which we currently do not have.

I don't know how long the current housing market malaise will last or how it will end, but I'm pretty confident that housing turnover (people moving from one place to another, which typically coincides with demand for storage units) will rebound eventually.

And now, here are the REIT preferred stocks I'm interested in buying:

Dividend Yield Upside To Par
Digital Realty 5.2% Preferred Series L (DLR.PR.L) 6.5% 24%
Regency Centers 6.25% Series A (REGCP) 6.7% 8%
Rexford Industrial 5.625% Series C (REXR.PR.C) 6.7% 18%

To be clear, I don't think upside to par matters that much because the DLR and REXR prefs are unlikely to be called anytime soon, while the REG pref could be called if interest rates decline somewhat, making it worthwhile to swap the 6.25% yield for a long-term bond at a lower interest rate.

You can find higher-yielding preferred stocks, including from REITs, but I'd argue that the risk-reward profile of these three REIT prefs is attractive. All three are investment-grade-rated REITs with relatively low leverage, conservative financial management, decent-or-better growth prospects, and high-quality property portfolios.

  • DLR owns and develops data centers, including some leased to hyperscalers.
  • REG owns Class A, mostly grocery-anchored shopping centers in prime locations across the country.
  • REXR owns highly functional infill industrial buildings in the supply-constrained region of Southern California.

Generating 6.5-6.7% yields from such strong and reliable REITs strikes me as a compelling opportunity.

Plus, each of them is trading near their lowest prices since their respective IPOs:

As you might have noticed from the images above, trading volume is something to keep in mind. During the last trading day, REXR.PR.C saw a grand total of 616 shares trade hands.

I certainly plan to use limit orders if and when I buy these prefs.

Over the next week, however, my incoming cash will be limited, so I may not be able to actually purchase any of these REIT prefs until the following weeks.

In any case, they are on my radar, so I thought I would share the ideas with those hunting for some safe yield.

data.title: Our Top 10 High Growth Dividend Stocks - June 2025
data.description:
Note: The stocks shortlisted and highlighted in this article are not buy recommendations per se but rather candidates for further research. Before making any investments, please use due diligence, considering your personal goals and risk tolerance. Also, some sections in the article may be repetitive from month to month for the benefit of the new readers. Such sections would be displayed in "italics," and regular readers could skip them. However, we recommend that first-time readers refer to our BLOG POST here to get a detailed explanation of our selection methodology/process and additional criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks.

Introduction:

Every month, we start with about 400 to 500 dividend-paying stocks and use our proprietary and unique filtering criteria to bring down the number of selections to a small subset of about 20 stocks. Generally, we end up with stocks that pay dividend yields in the range of 1% to 1.5%, which is in line with what the S&P 500 (SP500) pays as well. These 20 stocks are from diverse sectors and industries, but they all have one aspect in common. They appear to be in their period of hyper-growth with great momentum. As a final step, we analyze each of the 20 stocks and use our judgment to come up with a final list of just 10 stocks. The majority of our selections (if not all) have a market cap of $10 billion or more.

Who should follow this series?

The stocks highlighted in this series are for investors who are in their accumulation phase and are more interested in growing their capital at a rapid pace rather than their current income. The focus of the selections is dividend growth and growth sustainability rather than high current yield. In that sense, the risk profile of these stocks is generally higher, and they are not suitable for highly conservative investors or investors who need high current incomes to sustain their lifestyle.

Market Outlook:

The market has continued to gain momentum this past month, building on the gains in the previous 30 days. The S&P 500 crossed the psychological threshold level of 6000, though it was previously achieved back in Nov. 2024.

Obviously, many things have happened between now and early April. Back then, the market panic was about tariffs and the disruptions they may bring. However, that tariff fear has more or less evaporated as the Trump administration has shown that it is mostly using tariffs as a negotiation tactic and intends to be flexible when needed. That said, it is likely that a base tariff of 10% would stay almost on every country, but that may not be such a bad thing and, in fact, may help in bringing down the trade deficit in the long run. Even though only one country (the U.K.) has finalized the trade deal so far, many others are progressing. The Trump administration has indicated that it is open to extending the 90-day pause for those where a deal is being worked out in good faith.

On the geopolitical front, the old wars are still raging, and a new one has just started between Israel and Iran. Obviously, the oil prices (CL1:COM) have shot up, but other than that, the market has taken it in its stride for now. Also, so far, the U.S. has stayed away from direct involvement, which is a positive for the market. However, that can change at any moment.

The Fed is still holding rates steady between 4.25% and 4.50%, at least until now (as of Jun.17). Most market participants (nearly 99%) see the Fed doing nothing and staying pat during its next FOMC (Federal Open Market Committee) meeting on Jun. 18th. The earliest rate cut is expected in Sep. or Oct. 2025. In our view, the Fed is behind the curve and late in cutting rates as usual. Inflation is tame, and the relatively high interest rates are causing a lot of problems for the broader economy, especially for housing. However, it is entirely possible that the Fed will surprise the market with a 25-basis point rate cut on Jun. 18th, which will provide a big boost to the market as well as to strapped-up consumers.

You can see the odds of the Fed's next rate cut here on the CME FedWatchTool website.

We can never be sure of the future direction of the markets. However, we intend to stay invested in stocks that have the best chance of growing rapidly in a bull market and not losing as much in a down market. The purpose of this series of articles is to highlight an outstanding list of high-growth stocks that also happen to have positive momentum behind them.

For more details and background information on this series, please read our blog post (the link provided at the beginning of the article).

Selection Criteria for Dividend Stocks:

This section briefly describes our selection criteria and process. For detailed information, please refer to the blog post (link provided earlier). We define our basic selection criteria here to bring our target list to roughly 400 stocks. Based on these criteria, we also calculate a "Dividend Safety Quality Score." In brief, the Quality Score is derived using the following factors:

  • Current Yield
  • Dividend growth history (number of years of dividend growth):
  • Payout ratio - Preferably based on Free Cash Flow.
  • Past five-year and 10-year dividend growth
  • Chowder number - the sum of the 5-yr dividend growth rate and the current yield
  • EPS growth (average of the previous five years of growth and expected next 3-5 years' growth)
  • Debt/equity and Debt/asset ratios
  • S&P's credit ratings (Standard & Poor's Global Ratings)
  • Distance from 52-week high (current price minus 52-week high price)
  • Sales or Revenue growth for the past five years.

Additional Criteria for Dividend Growth Stocks

We will now use the following additional criteria to filter out stocks that would fit the mold of high-growth DGI stocks.

  • The payment ratio (on a cash-flow basis or EPS basis) is less than 95% but preferably less than 80%.
  • 5-year annualized dividend growth (or 10-year growth rate) is at least 7.5% or greater. However, some exceptions can be made.
  • Chowder number (5-year dividend growth rate plus the current dividend yield) >= 9.

Until this stage of filtering, we kept the base criteria loose enough to allow a wide variety of stocks to make the list. However, we will perform additional filtering in the next stage to get the best candidates.

Most growth investors appreciate how important the earnings growth (earnings per share - EPS) is for a stock to grow its dividend rapidly. Without growth in earnings, the company cannot grow its dividends sustainably for long. So, our focus ought to be on earnings growth.

In our spreadsheet, we will add four more columns of data for each of the stocks:

  • EPS (earnings per share) Quality Rating
  • Last Qtr EPS change % % (actual)
  • Current Qtr EPS change % (est.)
  • Current year EPS change % (est.).

We will now assign weights to these four sets of data for each stock and add them to the original "Dividend Safety Quality Score" to come up with a modified Quality Score tilted in favor of high dividend growth stocks. We will call this column "High-Growth Quality Score."

Narrowing Down the List To 20 Stocks

The criteria listed above provide us with a list of roughly 350-400 stocks to work with. We will now filter it down and select roughly 50 stocks based on the following methodology.

  • We first select the top 20 stocks based on the highest High Growth-Quality-Score (adjusted for sector or industry over concentration). Please see Table-1A below.
  • We then take the top 10 stocks based on the highest 5-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1B below.
  • We will also select the top 10 stocks based on the highest 10-year dividend growth. Please see Table-1C below.
  • Finally, we will select the top 10 stocks based on the average of EPS Rating (earnings per share) and RS rating (Relative Strength). (EPS and RS ratings are taken from IBD; subscription required). Please see Table-1D below.

Table-1A: Top 20 Highest HG-Quality-Score Stocks:

Table-1B: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (5 years):

Table-1C: Top 10 Highest Past Dividend Growth Stocks (10 years):

Table-1D: Top 10 Stocks with Highest EPS and RS Rating:

We will now combine these 50 stocks from the above four tables and remove any duplicates from this combined list, as some stocks may have qualified based on multiple criteria.

The following stocks appeared two times: APH, AVGO, CI, CTAS, CTRA, FIX, MS, and NRG (8 duplicates).

Appeared three times: HWM (2 duplicates).

We are now left with 40 (50 - 10) names.

We will also remove any names where the revenue growth (over the past five years) has been negative. There is none on the list.

We will now calculate the average of EPS and RS (relative strength) ratings and remove any names that have an average of less than 60. This removes DKS, MOS, QSR, CI, and VST (leaving us 35 names).

We will also remove any names with both the EPS and RS less than 80. This removes OVV and SF, leaving us with 33 names.

Next, we will remove any stock that has an RS rating of less than 50. There is none on the list, leaving us still with 33 names.

Finally, we will need to ensure that there are not too many stocks from the same industry segment (or even a sector). In cases where there is overconcentration, we will keep the best ones that also have some momentum and tailwind. During this process, we prioritize the stocks that have higher Relative Strength and EPS ratings.

Finance-Bank-major: We keep SAN but remove BK and ZION.

Finance – Inv. Banking and others: We will keep MS but remove MA and V.

Computer Software: We will keep ORCL and INTU but remove MSFT and MSCI.

Computer Electronics: We will keep APH.

Computer- Semiconductors: We keep AVGO but remove TSM.

Industrial- Const. & Engg.: We keep PWR.

Industrial- Aerospace & Def.: We keep HWM but remove CW.

Gold: We keep RGLD.

Utility: We keep NRG and NFG.

Energy: We keep IMO but remove CTRA.

REITs: We remove AMH and EQIX.

We also remove some of the stocks with the lowest relative strength in the list. We also remove MLI and LLY.

In all, we removed eleven names, so we are finally left with 20 (33-13) names, which are presented in the table below (in order of the sector):

(FIX), (SAN), (INTU), (ORCL), (RL), (PWR), (APH), (CF), (MS), (HWM), (RGLD), (MCK), (WPM), (IMO), (WING), (AVGO), (PM), (CTAS), (NRG), and (NFG).

Table 2: Top 20 High-Growth DGI Stocks of the Month:

Final Step: Selection of Top 10 High-Growth DGI Stocks

Until now, we have mostly used our rule-based filtering criteria to come to a list of 20 stocks. However, we carefully avoided the high concentration of any one sector or industry segment. Now, this final step of bringing down the number of selections to just ten stocks is mostly a subjective process based on our judgment. We try to keep the group as diverse as possible, representing many sectors and industry segments. We encourage readers to look at the wider selection of 20 stocks and see if they come up with anything different and appropriate for their goals. Nonetheless, we describe below how we go about selecting these ten stocks for the month.

We sort our list of 20 stocks by sector and industry segment, then in decreasing order of high-growth quality score. We try to select one stock from each industry segment from top to bottom; however, at times, multiple stocks could appear from the same sector. We will try to keep the best stock in each industry segment based on multiple factors, such as the quality score and the recent momentum. We also need to recognize that this is "not" a permanent buy-and-hold portfolio, and some stocks get reshuffled every month, so the focus is on selecting stocks that are likely to do well on a short- to medium-term basis. That's why we are also going to give priority to stocks with solid EPS ratings and high positive momentum (Relative strength).

We want to caution that valuation is not a factor in the selection of these stocks, so some of these may be highly overvalued. Due to high valuation, their yields tend to be lower side as well, in the range of 1% to 2%.

Here are our top 10 selections for this month.

Current Month Selections (Jun. 2025):

(FIX), (SAN), (ORCL), (PWR), (APH), (HWM), (WPM), (AVGO), (PM), and (NFG).

Previous Month Selections:

(CTVA), (BCS), (MSFT), (STN), (APH), (HWM), (RGLD), (MCK), (AVGO), and (NRG).

Since our methodology is largely based on a filtering process, we would like to point out that once a stock has appeared on our list, it may repeat for at least a couple of months.
